questions.question: The principle of standard precautions is to:
options: Use PPE only for known infections
Treat all patients' blood and body fluids as potentially infectious
questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply universally because infection status is often unknown.

questions.question: Which is a component of standard precautions?
options: Hand hygiene and gloves, with eye/face/gown protection when splashing is possible
questions.rationale: Hand hygiene and gloves are baseline, with added barriers when splash exposure is possible.

questions.question: The three most important bloodborne viral illnesses for providers are:
questions.rationale: Hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and HIV are the key bloodborne viral risks.

questions.question: Which bloodborne virus is vaccine-preventable?
questions.rationale: Hepatitis B has an effective vaccine; HCV and HIV do not.

questions.question: Of HBV, HCV, and HIV, which is the most transmissible?
questions.rationale: Hepatitis B is the most readily transmitted of the three.

questions.question: Bloodborne viruses are transmitted to providers primarily by:
options: Needlesticks, mucous-membrane splashes, and non-intact skin contact
questions.rationale: Transmission occurs via blood/fluids through needlesticks, mucous membranes, or broken skin.

questions.question: Droplet precautions (surgical mask) are used for:
questions.rationale: Large-droplet diseases like influenza and meningococcus require a surgical mask.

questions.question: Airborne precautions with a fit-tested N95 are required for:
questions.rationale: Small-particle airborne diseases (TB, measles, varicella) require N95 respirators.

questions.question: Contact precautions involve:
options: Gown and gloves for touch-spread organisms
questions.rationale: Contact precautions add a gown and gloves for organisms spread by touch.

questions.question: Symptoms suggesting tuberculosis include:
options: Chronic cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss
questions.rationale: Chronic cough with fever, night sweats, and weight loss should prompt airborne precautions for possible TB.

questions.question: The BCG vaccine can complicate TB screening because it:
options: Can cause a positive TB skin test
questions.rationale: BCG, used in some countries, can produce a positive skin test, requiring clinical interpretation.

questions.question: The first action after a needlestick exposure is to:
options: Ignore it if the patient looks healthy
Wash the site immediately with soap and water
Wait until the end of shift
questions.rationale: Immediately wash the site (flush mucous membranes), then report and seek evaluation.

questions.question: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is most effective when:
options: Started as soon as possible after exposure
questions.rationale: PEP is time-sensitive and most effective when begun within hours of exposure.

questions.question: Regarding hepatitis C after exposure:
options: There is no PEP, but early identification allows monitoring and treatment
questions.rationale: No PEP exists for HCV, but early detection enables monitoring and curative treatment if infection occurs.

questions.question: Why apply standard precautions to apparently 'low-risk' patients?
questions.rationale: Because many infections are unrecognized, universal precautions protect against unknown risks.

questions.question: After an exposure, the provider should:
options: Keep it private and do nothing
Report it per protocol and seek prompt evaluation
questions.rationale: Reporting and prompt evaluation enable timely PEP and follow-up testing.

questions.question: The correct PPE for a suspected measles patient is:
options: Fit-tested N95 for the provider and a surgical mask on the patient
questions.rationale: Measles is airborne—use an N95 and place a surgical mask on the patient.

questions.question: Hepatitis B-vaccinated providers exposed to blood are:
options: Strongly protected, with follow-up testing/prophylaxis as indicated
Required to start HIV PEP only
questions.rationale: HBV vaccination provides strong protection; follow-up and any needed prophylaxis are arranged based on status.
